返回本批次

洛杉矶陪审团做出具有里程碑意义的裁决：Instagram、YouTube 旨在让孩子上瘾

Landmark L.A. jury verdict finds Instagram, YouTube were designed to addict kids

www.latimes.com 作者 1vuio0pswjnm7 分数 298 评论 3 2026/03/26 20:15
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 在美国最受关注的民事审判之一中，经过七周的艰苦法庭诉讼和为期 9 天的 40 多个小时的紧张审议后，陪审团于周三在洛杉矶县高等法院做出了一项具有里程碑意义的裁决，认定 Instagram 和 YouTube 对加利福尼亚州奇科市一名妇女的痛苦负有责任，她指控这些平台是为了让年轻用户上瘾而建立的。 20 岁的原告 Kaley G.M. 于 2 月份出庭作证，上午 10 点左右到达法庭。宣读判决书、300 万美元的赔偿金和额外惩罚性赔偿的决定时，她仍然保持着坚忍不拔的态度。一位同伴强忍着泪水，下巴颤抖着。尽管卡罗琳·B·库尔法官一再警告不要回应，但仍有几名观察员默默哭泣。库尔警告说：“我们不需要对陪审团的裁决做出任何反应——不哭喊、不做出反应、不打扰。” “如果有的话，我们将不得不把你从法庭上驱逐出去，我们当然不想这样做。”在做出初步裁决后不到两小时，陪审团重新判决 Meta 获得 210 万美元的惩罚性赔偿，谷歌获得 90 万美元的惩罚性赔偿，从而使两家公司的判决总额达到 600 万美元。周三上午，Snapchat 和 TikTok 的律师也出庭听取了这一决定。审判前，这两个平台与 Kaley 达成庭外和解，具体金额不详。 Instagram 母公司 Meta 的发言人表示：“我们谨表示不同意这一判决，并正在评估我们的法律选择。”新墨西哥州陪审团裁定 Meta 对与州律师相关的 3.75 亿美元损失承担责任后不到 24 小时就做出了判决。劳尔·托雷兹 (Raúl Torrez) 将军声称 Instagram 变成了儿童掠夺者的“温床”，该平台誓言要对这一决定提出上诉。洛杉矶陪审团花了更长的时间来审议​​。周五，陪审员们在吃披萨的午休时间前询问库尔，是否所有人都应该权衡损害赔偿，或者只有那些同意承担责任的人。周一，他们告诉库尔，他们很难就其中一名被告达成一致。库尔告诉陪审团要继续努力。 Kaley 说她第一次迷上 YouTube 和 Instagram 是在小学的时候。陪审员负责确定这些公司在设​​计产品时是否存在疏忽行为，并且未能警告她存在危险。他们的判决将与数千起其他未决诉讼相呼应，重塑一些世界上最强大公司的法律格局。专家表示，这笔奖金可能会为未来的奖项设定标准。在此之前，特拉华州法院做出一项裁决，免除 Meta 的保险公司因“数千起有关其平台涉嫌对儿童造成伤害的诉讼”而造成的损失的责任，这一裁决可能会让 Meta 和其他科技巨头在未来承担数百万美元的责任。直到一月下旬开始的这次审判之前，陪审团还没有收到任何要求科技巨头对儿童伤害负责的诉讼。现在还有更多的事情即将发生。周三，受害者家属和支持者（从左到右）谢尔比·诺克斯、艾米·内维尔、玛丽·罗德、劳拉·马克斯-加勒特、莎拉·加德纳和列侬·托雷斯在洛杉矶高等法院外对判决作出反应。陪审团认定 Meta 和 YouTube 存在疏忽，裁定 Meta 对损害承担 70% 的责任，YouTube 对损害承担 30% 的责任，判给原告 300 万美元的损害赔偿。 （Kayla Bartkowski / 洛杉矶时报）Kaley 的测试案例是从目前在加州法院合并的数十起诉讼中选出的。还有数百人正在通过联邦系统一起转移，第一次审判定于 6 月在旧金山进行。总的来说，这些诉讼试图证明损害不是来自用户内容，而是来自平台本身的设计和运营。专家表示，这是一个重要的法律区别。迄今为止，社交媒体公司一直受到 1996 年一项名为“第 230 条”的强大法律的保护，该法律使这些应用程序无需对使用该应用程序的儿童所发生的事情承担责任。 Meta 和谷歌的律师认为，Kaley 的困境是她脾气暴躁的家庭生活以及新冠病毒大流行的影响的结果，而不是社交媒体。原告律师马克·拉尼尔周二在洛杉矶高等法院接受媒体采访。 （Kayla Bartkowski / 洛杉矶时报）“我认为它不应该进入陪审团审判，”加州大学伯克利分校法学院院长、第一修正案专家欧文·切默林斯基（Erwin Chemerinsky）说，该修正案也保护平台。 “所有媒体都试图让人们留在[他们的平台]并回来。”其他人则表示，社交媒体捕捉、培养和控制注意力的算法能力使其与适合青少年的浪漫小说、漫威电影或第一人称射击游戏有着根本的不同。佐治亚州立大学法学院教授埃里克·J·西格尔说：“这些案件确实非常棘手且令人心碎。” “它们代表了言论自由之间的冲突价值观以及保护那些为了盈利而从事言论自由放大的公司所造成的真正危害。” “让陪审员在没有更多指导的情况下解决所有这些问题很诱人，但也有风险，”他说。随着 3 月 13 日开始的审议持续进行，陪审员们也表达了类似的怀疑态度，要求查看 Meta 内部文件，并审查一名辩护专家的证词，“关于她的职业操守；作为唯一一位声称社交媒体不是影响 KGM 心理健康的因素的医生。”到周五，他们似乎就 Meta 的罪责达成了一致，但整个周二都在努力为谷歌做出决定，并在周三上午 10 点刚过就做出了裁决。 “今天，陪审团看到了真相，并要求 Meta 和谷歌对设计使儿童上瘾和伤害儿童的产品负责，”法院指定的相关联邦诉讼联合首席原告律师莱克西·哈扎姆 (Lexi Hazam) 表示。 “这一判决传达了一个明确的信息：没有一家公司可以逃脱责任。”这一结果可能会改变关于社交媒体成瘾这一概念、应用程序在设计成瘾方面可能发挥什么作用，以及像卡利这样的人是否能证明自己受到了困扰等已经很激烈的争论。这些平台的律师试图对这种疾病提出质疑——强调社交媒体成瘾没有正式的诊断——同时还辩称凯莉从未接受过治疗。 “用‘YouTube’这个词代替甲基苯丙胺这个词，”路易斯·李律师在周四的结案陈词中敦促陪审团。 “用你一生的经验问问自己，是否有一个患有毒瘾的人会说，‘是的，我只是失去了兴趣。’”“她坐在那里几个小时没有玩手机，”梅塔律师保罗·W·施密特(Paul W. Schmidt)说。 YouTube 团队还试图将这款视频分享应用与 Instagram 和其他社交媒体平台区分开来，称其功能根本不同。卡莉的团队称其为她社交媒体成瘾的“门户”。 “YouTube 并不是通往任何事物的门户，”李说。 “YouTube 是一个孩子喜欢然后就放下的玩具。” 陪审团不同意，最终认为该平台承担责任，尽管他们以 70 比 30 的比例分担了责任，其中 Meta 的权重较大。原告律师马克·拉尼尔（Mark Lanier）在整个审判过程中都展现了他德克萨斯州的淳朴民风，告诉陪审团他的心里话，并用油性铅笔在他的演示辅助工具上乱涂乱画。在对陪审团的直接讲话中，他使用了一套木制婴儿积木、一堆纸，甚至一把锤子和一箱鸡蛋。在周三上午晚些时候的审判惩罚阶段，他拿出一个装满 415 粒花生豆的玻璃罐，代表谷歌母公司 Alphabet 12 月份价值 4150 亿美元的股东权益。“你要为此对他们罚款什么？”他试探着。 “你要罚款他们十亿吗？”他从最上面的那堆巧克力豆中取出一颗绿色的巧克力豆。 “二十亿？”他又拿出另一个。 “你知道一包 M&M 巧克力里面有 18 颗 M&M 巧克力吗？你对他们罚款十亿美元，他们不会注意到。” “他们最不希望你做的事情就是谈论他们有多少 M&M 巧克力豆，”律师说道，并敦促陪审员“用 Meta 金钱与 Meta 交谈”。拉尼尔说：“他们最不希望你做的事情就是关注如何让他们对自己所做的事情负责。”相反，技术团队依靠流畅的数字演示来审查证据并阐明他们的论点。 “重点关注本案中存在争议的事实，”施密特在结案期间敦促陪审团。 “不是律师辩论，不是一杯水或一罐巧克力豆之类的道具，而是实际的证据。”在审判的惩罚阶段，他试图强调“无意伤害”儿童，并且公司一直在努力提高其产品的安全性。该案件是 Meta 首席执行官马克·扎克伯格首次出庭作证的案件，他为 Instagram 的安全记录进行了辩护，并对让年轻人远离该应用程序的难度表示遗憾。它还公开了数万页的内部文件——拉尼尔认为文件显示这些公司故意针对儿童，并设计他们的产品以使他们在平台上停留更长时间。拉尼尔在周四的结案陈词中对陪审团表示：“这些是你唯一能看到的内部文件，因为你是审理此案的陪审团。” “它让你获得了世界上从未有过的曝光度。”专家表示，这些先前未公开的材料可能对陪审团的最终裁决至关重要。加州中区前美国代理检察官、“技术相关伤害”专家约瑟夫·麦克纳利 (Joseph McNally) 表示，“这里的内部电子邮件很关键——他们描绘了 Meta 的冷漠景象。”专家表示，科技巨头已经发誓要对加州和新墨西哥州的判决提出上诉，几乎要确保该问题最终由最高法院裁决。更多阅读内容

Original Summary

  1. After a grueling seven weeks of court proceedings and more than 40 hours of tense deliberations across nine days in one of the country’s most closely watched civil trials, jurors handed down a landmark decision in Los Angeles County Superior Court on Wednesday, finding Instagram and YouTube responsible for the suffering of a Chico, Calif., woman who charged the platforms were built to addict young users. Kaley G.M., the 20-year-old plaintiff, who testified in February, arrived in court just before 10 a.m. She remained stoic as the verdict, an award of $3 million and a decision warranting additional punitive damages were read out. A companion fought back tears, her chin quivering. Several observers wept silently despite Judge Carolyn B. Kuhl’s repeated warning not to respond. “We need to have no reaction to the jury’s verdict — no crying out, no reactions, no disturbance,” Kuhl warned. “If there is we will have to have you removed from the courtroom, and we sure don’t want to have to do that.”Less than two hours after it delivered its initial verdict, the jury returned to award $2.1 million in punitive damages against Meta and $900,000 against Google, bringing the total judgment against the companies to $6 million combined. Attorneys for Snapchat and TikTok also appeared in court Wednesday morning to hear the decision. The two platforms settled with Kaley out of court for undisclosed sums before the trial. “We respectfully disagree with the verdict and are evaluating our legal options,” a spokesperson for Instagram’s parent company, Meta, said. The verdict arrived less than 24 hours after a New Mexico jury found Meta liable for $375 million in damages related to state Atty. Gen. Raúl Torrez’s claim it turned Instagram into a “breeding ground” for child predators — a decision the platform has vowed to appeal. The Los Angeles jury took much longer to deliberate. On Friday, jurors preempted their pizza lunch break to ask Kuhl whether all of them should weigh in on damages, or only those who’d agreed on liability. On Monday, they told Kuhl they were struggling to agree about one of the defendants. Kuhl told the jury to keep trying. Kaley said she first got hooked on YouTube and Instagram in grade school. Jurors were charged with determining whether the companies acted negligently in designing their products and failed to warn her of the dangers. Their verdict will echo through thousands of other pending lawsuits, reshaping the legal landscape for some of the world’s most powerful companies. Experts say the payout will likely set the bar for future awards. It comes on the heels of a Delaware court decision clearing Meta’s insurers of responsibility for damages incurred from “several thousand lawsuits regarding the harm its platforms allegedly cause children” — a ruling that could leave it and other tech titans on the hook for untold future millions. Until this trial, which began in late January, no suit seeking to hold tech titans responsible for harms to children had ever reached a jury. Many more are now set to follow. Victims’ families and supporters, left to right, Shelby Knox, Amy Neville, Mary Rodee, Laura Marquez-Garrett, Sarah Gardner and Lennon Torres react to the verdict outside the Los Angeles Superior Court on Wednesday. The jury found Meta and YouTube negligent, finding Meta 70% responsible for harm and YouTube 30% responsible awarding the plaintiff $3 million in damages. (Kayla Bartkowski / Los Angeles Times) Kaley’s test case was chosen from among scores of suits currently consolidated in California state court. Hundreds more are moving together through the federal system, where the first trial is set for June in San Francisco. Collectively, the suits seek to prove that harm flowed not from user content but from the design and operation of the platforms themselves. That’s a critical legal distinction, experts say. Social media companies have so far been protected by a powerful 1996 law called Section 230, which has shielded the apps from responsibility for what happens to children who use it. Lawyers for Meta and Google argued Kaley’s struggles were the result of her fractious home life and fallout from the COVID pandemic, not social media. Plaintiffs’ attorney Mark Lanier speaks with the media at the Los Angeles Superior Court on Tuesday. (Kayla Bartkowski / Los Angeles Times) “I don’t think it should have ever gotten to a jury trial,” said Erwin Chemerinsky, dean of the UC Berkeley School of Law and an expert on the 1st Amendment, which also protects the platforms. “All media tries to keep people on [their platform] and coming back.” Others say social media’s algorithmic ability to capture, cultivate and control attention makes it fundamentally different from teen-friendly romantasy novels, Marvel movies or first-person shooter games. “These are truly hard and heartbreaking cases,” said Eric J. Segall, a professor at Georgia State College of Law. “They represent a clash between free speech values and the real harms caused by protecting those companies that engage in free speech amplification for profit.” “Letting jurors sort all of this out without more guidance is tempting but also risky,” he said. As deliberations that began March 13 wore on, jurors signaled similar skepticism, asking to see internal Meta documents, and reviewing testimony from a defense expert “in regards to her professional integrity; being the only doctor stating social media was not a contributing factor to KGM’s mental health.” They appeared to agree on Meta’s culpability by Friday, but labored through Tuesday to hash out a decision for Google, delivering their verdict just after 10 a.m. Wednesday. “Today, a jury saw the truth and held Meta and Google accountable for designing products that addict and harm children,” said Lexi Hazam, court-appointed co-lead plaintiffs’ counsel in the related federal action. “This verdict sends an unmistakable message that no company is above accountability.” The outcome will likely transform the already heated debate over social media addiction as a concept, what role apps may play in engineering it, and whether individuals like Kaley can prove they’re afflicted. The platforms’ attorneys sought to cast doubt on the ailment — emphasizing that there is no formal diagnosis for social media addiction — while also arguing that Kaley had never been treated for it. “Substitute the words ‘YouTube’ for the word methamphetamine,” attorney Luis Li urged the jury during closing arguments Thursday. “Ask yourselves with your lifetime of experience whether anybody suffering from addiction could say, ‘Yeah, I just kind of lost interest.’” “She was sitting there for hours without being on her phone,” said Meta attorney Paul W. Schmidt. YouTube’s team also sought to distance the video-sharing app from Instagram and other social media platforms, saying its functions are fundamentally different. Kaley’s team called it “a gateway” to her social media addiction. “YouTube wasn’t a gateway to anything,” Li said. “YouTube was a toy that a child liked and then put down.”Jurors disagreed, ultimately holding the platform liable, though they split the liability 70-30, weighting heavily to Meta. Plaintiffs’ attorney Mark Lanier leaned on his down-home Texas folksiness throughout the trial, telling the jury what was on his heart and scribbling with grease pencil on his demonstrative aids. In his direct addresses to the jury, he used a set of wooden baby blocks, stacks of paper, even a hammer and a crate of eggs. During the punitive phase of the trial late Wednesday morning, he brought out a glass jar filled with 415 peanut M&Ms to represent the $415 billion of stockholder’s equity Google’s parent company, Alphabet, was valued at in December.“What are you going to fine them for this?” he probed. “Are you going to fine them a billion?” He plucked a green M&M from the top of the pile. “Two billion?” He pulled out another. “You know a pack of M&Ms has 18 M&Ms in it? You fine them a billion, and they’re not going to notice.” “The last thing in the world they want you to do is talk about how many M&Ms they’ve got,” the lawyer said, urging jurors to “talk to Meta in Meta money.” “The last thing in the world they want you to do is focus on what it takes to hold them accountable for what they’ve done,” Lanier said.Conversely, the tech teams relied on slick digital presentations to review evidence and illustrate their arguments. “Focus on those facts that are at issue in this case,” Schmidt urged the jury during closings. “Not lawyer arguments, not props like a glass of water or a jar of M&Ms, but actual proof in evidence.” During the punitive phase of the trial, he sought to emphasize that “there wasn’t an intention to do harm” to children, and that it had worked diligently to make its products safer.The case was the first to get Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg on the witness stand, where he defended Instagram’s safety record and lamented the difficulty of keeping youngsters off the app. It also made public tens of thousands of pages of internal documents — documents Lanier argued showed the companies intentionally targeted children, and engineered their products to keep them on the platforms longer. “These are internal documents that you’re uniquely seeing because you’re the jury that got to sit on this case,” Lanier told the jury during closing arguments on Thursday. “It’s given you exposure that the world hasn’t had.” Those previously undisclosed materials likely proved critical to the jury’s ultimate verdict, experts said. “Internal emails here were key — they painted a picture of indifference at Meta,” said Joseph McNally, former Acting U.S. Attorney for the Central District of California and an expert in “technology-related harm.”The tech titans have already vowed to appeal both the California and New Mexico verdicts, all-but ensuring the issue is ultimately decided by the Supreme Court, experts said. More to Read

philsnow 2026/03/27 04:20
thither 是英语中唯一标有夺格的单词之一

https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/ablative_case

thither is one of the only words in the english language that has the ablative case marked on it
